title: Data & Business Intelligence
simple_html:
Companies collect huge amounts of data, but raw data is useless until it is organized and analyzed. This lesson covers how data is stored in databases and how business intelligence (BI) turns that data into insights managers can act on.

Storing data: databases

A database is an organized collection of related data. Most businesses use a relational database, which stores data in tables (rows and columns) that can be linked together. This avoids the problems of older file systems, where the same data was duplicated in many places and easily became inconsistent.

From data to insight: business intelligence

  • Data warehouse — a large central store that pulls data from many systems and organizes it for analysis (not day-to-day operations).
  • Business intelligence — the tools and methods used to analyze that data: reports, dashboards, and visualizations.
  • Data mining — digging through large data sets to find hidden patterns, such as which products sell together.
  • Big data — extremely large, fast, varied data (from sensors, social media, clicks) needing special tools.

Why it matters

Good data management plus BI lets a company spot trends, understand customers, and make decisions based on evidence rather than guesswork.

Key takeaways

  • Databases organize data and reduce duplication.
  • Relational databases store data in linked tables.
  • Data warehouses centralize data for analysis.
  • BI and data mining turn data into actionable insight.
  • Big data needs new tools beyond traditional databases.
intermediate_html:
Data is a strategic asset, but only if it is well organized and analyzed. This lesson covers the two halves of that challenge: managing data with database technology, and extracting value from it with business intelligence and analytics.

Why databases beat file systems

Early systems stored data in separate files for each application, causing data redundancy (the same fact stored many times), data inconsistency (copies disagree), and tight program-data dependence. A database solves these by storing data centrally and serving many applications through a database management system (DBMS), which controls access, enforces rules, and minimizes redundancy.

The relational model

The dominant approach is the relational database, which organizes data into tables (relations) of rows (records) and columns (fields, or attributes). Each row is identified by a primary key, and tables are linked through foreign keys. Normalization is the process of designing tables to eliminate redundancy and update anomalies. Users query the data with SQL (Structured Query Language). Relational databases enforce ACID reliability — vital for the transaction processing systems of Lesson 3.

OLTP vs. OLAP

Operational databases are optimized for OLTP (Online Transaction Processing): many small, fast read/write transactions. Analysis needs the opposite — OLAP (Online Analytical Processing): complex queries scanning large volumes across many dimensions (time, region, product). Running heavy analysis on the live operational database would slow it down, which is why firms separate the two.

The data warehouse architecture

A data warehouse is a central repository that consolidates data from many operational systems into a consistent, historical, analysis-ready form. Data is loaded through an ETL process (Extract, Transform, Load) that cleans and standardizes it. A data mart is a smaller, department-focused subset of a warehouse (e.g., a marketing data mart). This architecture gives the firm a single, trustworthy source for analysis.

Business intelligence and analytics

Built on the warehouse, business intelligence (BI) comprises the tools that deliver information to decision-makers: reports, queries, dashboards, and visualizations. Business analytics goes further, applying statistical and modeling techniques. A useful ladder of analytics: descriptive (what happened?), diagnostic (why?), predictive (what is likely to happen?), and prescriptive (what should we do?). Data mining finds hidden patterns — associations ("customers who buy X also buy Y"), classifications, clusters, and forecasts.

Worked mini-case

A supermarket chain runs its tills on an OLTP relational database. Each night, ETL loads transactions into a data warehouse. A marketing data mart supports the analytics team. Using data mining, they discover an association: shoppers who buy nappies on weekday evenings often also buy ready meals. This descriptive/diagnostic insight feeds a predictive model of basket value and a prescriptive recommendation to co-locate the products and target a coupon. A BI dashboard then tracks the uplift. The chain has moved from raw transactions to a profit-improving action.

Big data, NoSQL, and how to apply it

Traditional relational databases struggle with big data — data of high volume, velocity, and variety (the "3 Vs"), such as social media, sensor/IoT, and clickstream data, much of it unstructured. NoSQL databases and distributed frameworks handle this scale and flexibility. To apply the lesson: (1) keep operational (OLTP) and analytical (OLAP/warehouse) systems separate; (2) invest in data quality and governance, because dirty data ruins analysis ("garbage in, garbage out"); (3) match the analytics type to the question (descriptive through prescriptive); and (4) choose the storage technology to fit the data — relational for structured transactions, NoSQL/big-data platforms for high-variety, high-volume streams.
full_html:
Data is the foundational raw material of every information system and, when well managed and analyzed, a genuine source of competitive advantage (Lesson 2). This lesson treats the full arc: organizing data with database technology, consolidating it for analysis in data warehouses, and extracting value through business intelligence, analytics, and data mining — including the big-data technologies reshaping the field. It carries graduate-level rigor and connects directly to the systems typology of Lesson 3.

The file-system problem and the database solution

The traditional file environment, in which each application maintained its own data files, produced four classic pathologies: data redundancy (duplicate storage), data inconsistency (redundant copies diverge), program-data dependence (changing storage breaks programs), and poor data sharing and security. The database approach centralizes data and interposes a database management system (DBMS) between applications and data, providing data independence, controlled redundancy, integrity enforcement, concurrency control, security, and a common query interface. The DBMS is one of the most consequential inventions in computing.

The relational model and database design

Codd (1970), at IBM, introduced the relational model, representing data as mathematical relations — tables of tuples (rows/records) and attributes (columns/fields) — manipulated by relational algebra. Its elegance and theoretical grounding made it dominant. Key constructs: a primary key uniquely identifies each row; a foreign key establishes referential links between tables; SQL is the standard declarative query language. Sound design proceeds via entity-relationship (ER) modeling (Chen, 1976) to identify entities, attributes, and relationships, then normalization to progressively higher normal forms (1NF through 3NF and beyond) to eliminate redundancy and update, insertion, and deletion anomalies. Relational DBMSs guarantee ACID properties (Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability), the reliability bedrock for the transaction processing systems of Lesson 3.

OLTP versus OLAP: two opposing workloads

DimensionOLTP (operational)OLAP (analytical)
PurposeRun the businessAnalyze the business
OperationsMany short read/write transactionsFew complex, read-heavy queries
DesignNormalized for integrityDenormalized (star/snowflake) for query speed
DataCurrent, detailedHistorical, aggregated, multidimensional

Because these workloads conflict, firms physically separate them — running analytics against operational systems degrades transaction performance and risks inconsistency. This separation motivates the data warehouse.

Data warehousing and the analytical architecture

Inmon (1992), often called the father of the data warehouse, defined it as a subject-oriented, integrated, time-variant, and non-volatile collection of data supporting management decision-making. Data is consolidated from many sources via ETL (Extract, Transform, Load) — extracting from operational systems, transforming (cleansing, standardizing, integrating), and loading into the warehouse; the modern ELT variant defers transformation to scalable target platforms. Kimball (1996) advanced the complementary dimensional modeling approach using star schemas (a central fact table surrounded by dimension tables) optimized for OLAP. A data mart is a departmental subset of the warehouse. More recently, the data lake stores raw structured and unstructured data at scale for flexible later use, and the data lakehouse seeks to combine warehouse governance with lake flexibility.

From data to decisions: BI and the analytics ladder

Davenport (2006), in "Competing on Analytics," argued that analytics capability is itself a strategic differentiator. Business intelligence denotes the infrastructure and tools (reporting, querying, dashboards, OLAP, visualization) that deliver information to decision-makers; business analytics emphasizes the statistical and modeling techniques that generate forward-looking insight. A widely used maturity ladder:

  • Descriptive analytics — what happened? (reports, dashboards, KPIs)
  • Diagnostic analytics — why did it happen? (drill-down, correlation)
  • Predictive analytics — what is likely to happen? (regression, classification, machine learning; Lesson 8 and AI topics)
  • Prescriptive analytics — what should we do? (optimization, simulation, recommendation)

Data mining

Fayyad, Piatetsky-Shapiro & Smyth (1996) framed data mining within the broader knowledge discovery in databases (KDD) process. Core tasks include association (market-basket analysis — the apocryphal "beer and nappies" finding), classification (assigning items to predefined categories, e.g., creditworthy/not), clustering (discovering natural groupings, e.g., customer segments), sequence/forecasting, and anomaly detection. These techniques power analytical CRM (Lesson 4), fraud detection, and recommendation engines.

Big data and NoSQL

The big-data era is characterized by the three Vs (Laney, 2001): Volume (petabyte scale), Velocity (streaming, real-time), and Variety (structured, semi-structured, and unstructured data from social media, sensors/IoT, clickstreams, text, images); later extensions add Veracity (data quality/uncertainty) and Value. Such data overwhelms traditional relational systems, motivating distributed frameworks (Hadoop's MapReduce, then Apache Spark) and NoSQL databases — key-value, document, column-family, and graph stores — that trade strict ACID consistency for horizontal scalability and schema flexibility, governed by the CAP theorem's consistency-availability-partition-tolerance trade-offs. The strategic implication: data variety and scale enable network-effect advantages (Lesson 2), but only for firms that build the requisite analytical capability.

Worked example: an end-to-end analytics pipeline

A retail bank seeks to reduce credit-card churn. Storage: transactions live in a normalized OLTP relational database. Consolidation: nightly ETL loads them, with demographics and call-center logs, into a star-schema data warehouse; a risk data mart serves the analytics team. Analysis: descriptive dashboards show rising churn; diagnostic drill-down isolates the affected segment; a predictive classification model (trained on historical churners) scores each customer's churn probability; clustering reveals distinct at-risk personas. Action: prescriptive optimization allocates a limited retention budget to the highest-value, highest-risk customers, and a BI dashboard monitors the campaign. The bank has traversed the full path from raw data to measured outcome — provided the underlying data is clean.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • "More data automatically yields better decisions." Without quality and governance, more data magnifies error — garbage in, garbage out. Master data management and data governance are prerequisites, not afterthoughts.
  • Running analytics on the operational database. This conflates OLTP and OLAP, degrading both; separation is foundational.
  • Confusing correlation with causation in data mining. Patterns suggest hypotheses; they do not establish cause, and acting naively on spurious correlations is a classic error.
  • Treating big data as a technology purchase. The advantage comes from analytical talent and a question-driven culture, not the platform alone (the complementary-assets theme again).
  • Believing relational is always right. Highly variable, unstructured, or extreme-scale data is often better served by NoSQL or lake architectures.

Cross-links

This lesson supplies the data foundation underpinning the TPS/MIS/DSS/ESS systems of Lesson 3 and the analytical layers of the enterprise applications in Lesson 4 (analytical CRM, supply-chain analytics). Its data-quality and governance themes connect to the security and control concerns of Lesson 7 and the ethics and privacy issues of Lesson 8, while its predictive techniques lead into machine learning and AI in business.
